data.title: China Reaffirms Tight Grip On Gold Market, Ushering In A New Monetary Era
data.description:
Without a doubt, the Chinese central bank (PBoC) is still the leading single entity that is driving up the gold price to record highs, year-to-date by more than 55 percent.

In the third quarter of 2025, the PBoC’s gold purchases (reported and unreported) accounted for 118 tonnes, up 39% MoM and 55% YoY, according to my long-time methodology1 (now copied by Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg, MarketWatch, The Washington Post, TIME magazine, Financial Times, Financiele Dagblad, and El País, to name a few).

Chart 1. Reported central bank gold buying versus estimates by the World Gold Council on central bank gold buying based on field research (reported and unreported buying). The majority of unreported buying must be ascribed to China.

My estimated total for Chinese monetary gold reserves stands at 5,411 tonnes in Q3, versus 2,304 tonnes reported by the central bank of China to the IMF.

Why is China buying so much gold?

It’s because China is the second-largest economy globally, and due to the weaponization of the dollar since the Ukraine war in 2022, the vast, covert buying spree by China and countries like Saudi Arabia should not be viewed as a hedge against the dollar but as a replacement for the dollar.

The mBridge Gold Standard

For the better part of the past 80 years, the U.S. dollar has functioned as the world’s trade and reserve currency. This setup gave the United States the exorbitant privilege of being able to print money to pay for imports, even though America’s manufacturing base has been eroded as a consequence.

China aims to establish an alternative to the U.S. dollar while seeking to avoid the risks associated with issuing its own reserve currency.

On November 19, 2025, the chairman of the Central Bank of the United Arab Emirates completed a landmark digital currency transaction during a meeting with the governor of the People’s Bank of China, formally inaugurating project mBridge.

The platform allows participating countries with established digital currencies to conduct bilateral trade in their own currencies, bypassing the U.S. dollar.

For the “mBridge gold standard” to be fulfilled, any surplus of local currency accumulated through trade must be directly exchangeable in a liquid gold market. Furthermore, it requires a new international gold vaulting and clearinghouse network.

Chart 2. In a historical shift, Eastern countries that before 2022 were price sensitive are now driving gold higher.

China will be the largest, or one of the largest, trading partners of countries participating in mBridge, and so the renminbi will be a dominant trade currency in the arrangement. This is why the Chinese are developing the Shanghai International Gold Exchange (SGEI).

The SGEI was launched in the Shanghai Free Trade Zone (FTZ) in 2014 and recently opened its first offshore vault in Hong Kong. The idea is to open additional vaults abroad.

The international vaulting system will likely be developed in accordance with the extensive network of repositories that constitute the SGEI system within the Chinese domestic market.

In the last SGEI Annual Report, it states that the SGEI adopts a “centralization, netting, and multi-tiered” clearing model with 18 certified margin custodian banks offering 70 certified vaults in 36 cities throughout the mainland.

Gold Is Shifting the Distribution of Global Reserves

As central banks aggressively load up on gold and move the price to new all-time highs, global gold reserves are rising to the detriment of the dollar. By Q3, my estimated total of central banks’ monetary gold reserves accounted for 40,225 tonnes, 49 percent of which is owned by countries outside the West. In the 1990s, this percentage was 25 percent.

Chart 3. The West comprises the US, UK, Western Europe, and Australia.

Due to the actions of the PBoC in increasing prices, monetary gold now accounts for almost 28 percent of global international reserves. This is a truly monumental increase from 21 percent in 2024.

Chart 4. Because gold has appreciated against all reserve currencies since Q3, gold’s “live” share of reserves is actually closer to 30 percent.

Notes

  • My most important analyses on the covert PBoC’s gold buying are:
  • Estimating the True Size of China’s Gold Reserves (February 2023)
  • PBoC Gold Conduit Revealed—Chinese Central Bank Did Not Stop Buying Gold in May (July 2024)
  • Chinese Central Bank Just Secretly Bought 60 Tonnes of Gold (November 2024)
  • China’s Gold Reserves Going Through the Roof (April 2025)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Altria: 40 Big Yields Down Big, These 4 Worth Considering
data.description:
When it comes to income-focused investing, not all big yields are created equally. And from four distinct big-yield categories (including (1) declining businesses, (2) manufactured distributions, (3) selling shares, and (4) well-covered qualified dividend yields), Altria’s (MO) category is arguably the best (it’s in group four), and the shares are currently down significantly and worth considering. This report shares data on 40 big yields (with share prices currently down big), reviews four attractive investment opportunities (from across the four groups), dives deeper into Altria (including a review of the business, sin-stock valuation discount, dividend safety, and risks), and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

40 Big Yields Down Big

For starters, here is a look at 40 popular big-yield investment opportunities, all of them recently down significantly (as compared to the S&P 500 (SPY)).

But before you go blindly buying any of these names, just because they are trading lower, let’s briefly review some important characteristics of each of the four big-yield types (mentioned earlier) to help you make more informed investment decisions.

Declining Businesses

All else equal, when a share price falls, the dividend yield mathematically increases. And just because the share price is down, that doesn’t necessarily mean it’s a good investment. Rather, a lot of times it just means it’s a business in decline. For some perspective, let’s consider the 10 biggest dividend yields in the S&P 500 (see below). Pay close attention to the 5- and 10-year total returns on these stocks versus the benchmark S&P (they are dramatically underperforming--arguably because they are businesses in decline).

If you had bought this entire group of 10 top dividend yields just because the yields were big, you might be sorely disappointed with your longer-term performance (again, the longer-term performance has been pretty terrible versus the S&P 500, as you can see above).

Manufactured Distributions

Next on our list, “manufactured distributions” include companies or investment funds (such as popular big-yield closed-end funds, or “CEFs”) that often support their big distribution yields with capital gains, heavy borrowing, derivative instruments, and/or a return of your own investment dollars (which all add a layer of risk to your investment, not to mention potential tax consequences (more on this later)). Here are ten examples (of big, manufactured distributions, all with share prices down significantly) in the table below.

This is not to suggest temporary actions to support the dividend, or CEFs entirely, are bad. They are not. But manufactured distributions can be a bit artificial and do introduce new risks investors need to consider, such as the specific CEF questions I propose in the graphic below.

We'll return to these questions (and the specific big yields in the table) later in this report.

Selling Shares (to Generate Income):

A common source of consternation for many income-focused investors is when some young hotshot with little to lose suggests all income investors would be better off if they just bought great growth stocks and then sold a few shares every month or quarter to generate whatever spending cash they need to support their lifestyle.

And while this can be a good strategy, in some situations, to generate some of your income needs, it’s also often reckless and ignorant advice that can quickly send you to the poorhouse if the market crashes (like it did in the tech bubble, the Great Financial Crisis, the initial Covid crash, and the subsequent post-Covid stimulus growth stock bubble that burst hard) and you’re forced to sell shares at the bottom to raise cash and thereby permanently locking in your losses—yuck!

For example, you can see examples (in the chart above) of just how far top growth stocks and the overall market can actually fall during periods of distress. Not to mention the unnecessary stress and lost sleep fretting and worrying about dramatically inconsistent levels of market volatility (not pleasant!).

Well-Covered Qualified Big Dividends

Arguably the best big-yield category is the special group of well-covered, qualified big dividend payers supported by financially strong businesses that are likely to keep performing well, regardless of whatever crisis the market faces next (Altria is an example of one such attractive dividend, as will be covered in subsequent sections of this report).

For instance, you can see examples of ten top dividend stocks currently down big included within the popular Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) in the table below (this fund is known for dividend safety and quality).

Not only do these individual companies avoid risks commonly associated with declining businesses (the fund screens against many such risks), as well as avoiding manufactured distributions and living and dying by market volatility (e.g., selling shares for income as discussed earlier), but they are also “qualified” dividends, which means they may be taxed at a lower rate, depending on your own tax bracket and whether you own them in a taxable account or a tax-advantaged retirement account.

This can be a huge advantage over other popular big-yield categories, such as CEFs, BDCs, and REITs, for example (their dividends are often actually “distributions” that can be taxed at a higher rate and/or lead to surprise capital gains tax bills when you sell in the future if they are relying on a significant return of your own capital to support the regular income payouts).

Qualified dividends can also provide tax advantages over master limited partnerships, or MLPs (another popular big-yield category), which can actually cause surprise tax consequences in otherwise tax-advantaged individual retirement accounts (IRAs) via unrelated business taxable income and painful K-1 tax statements delaying your individual tax return—yuck again!

And qualified dividends can also be more attractive than municipal bond (and municipal bond CEF) investments (which are often almost entirely tax exempt) after factoring in “tax-equivalent” total returns and the potential for ongoing long-term compound growth. Overall, big, qualified dividends can be a very special thing—depending on your own personal situation.

Altria Overview

One particularly interesting “qualified” big dividend (current yield is 7.2%) is Altria Group, especially considering the shares have recently sold off and the valuation is increasingly attractive.

About Altria:

Altria is a US-based holding company focused mainly on tobacco and related products. Specifically, Altria provides nicotine choices (to adult consumers 21+) through subsidiaries such as Philip Morris USA (cigarettes), U.S. Smokeless Tobacco Company (smokeless tobacco), John Middleton Co. (cigars), and NJOY LLC (e-vapor). Altria manufactures and sells premium brands such as Marlboro cigarettes, Copenhagen dip, and on! pouches, and Black & Mild cigars. It also has a strategic investment in Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) (~10% stake) and Cronos Group (CRON) (cannabis).

For some color, Altria generated $15.1 billion in revenue through the first 9 months of 2025 through the following three business segments:

  • Smokeable Products (88% of revenue): This is the core legacy business and includes combustible cigarettes (e.g., Marlboro) and machine-made large cigars (e.g., Black & Mild).

  • Oral Tobacco Products (12% of revenue): This encompasses moist smokeless tobacco (e.g., Copenhagen, Skoal) and oral nicotine pouches (e.g., on!).

  • All Other (0% of revenue): This includes e-vapor products (NJOY), heated tobacco sticks (via Horizon Innovations joint venture), oral nicotine innovation (Helix), and R&D for emerging platforms.

Altria’s Moat:

As a business, Altria has a wide moat versus the competition, stemming mainly from the addictive nature of its products and regulatory barriers to entry.

For example, Altria’s core product, nicotine, is highly addictive and commands strong brand loyalty. For instance, Altria's flagship Marlboro brand commands approximately 41-42% of the US cigarette market thanks to decades of brand equity that drives strong pricing power (well above inflation and any volume declines—more on this risk later).

And from a regulatory standpoint, things like strict FDA approvals for new or modified tobacco products and advertising bans (since the 1970s) make it nearly impossible for new competitors to enter the market successfully.

Altria’s “Sin-Stock” Valuation Discount

From a valuation standpoint, Altria is a financial powerhouse that is being significantly undervalued by the market (arguably due to “sin-stock” selling pressure from large “socially conscious” institutions) and thereby creating one of the most compelling dividend stock opportunities in the market.

Currently trading at just 10.5x forward earnings (see earlier table) and easily returning a mountain of free cash flow to investors (via qualified dividends and share repurchases), Altria is extremely compelling if you can get past the “sin-stock” nature of the product (nicotine is addictive, and the products are known to cause cancer and can discolor your teeth).

For some color, social screens within ESG mandates that prohibit large institutional investors (such as pension funds and endowments) from owning tobacco stocks are putting significant pressure on the shares (i.e., this significantly reduces the buyer pool). Studies show (for example, here and here) sin stocks like Altria exhibit 5-10% lower institutional holdings compared to similar non-sin peers, creating a persistent "sin discount" that suppresses valuations despite robust free cash flow generation ($9B+ annually).

Dividend Safety:

This structural bias creates an outstanding opportunity for unconstrained income investors to purchase shares at a discounted price and thereby mathematically boost their yield on cost. Discounted cash flow analysis from Morningstar suggests the shares are currently 10% undervalued—which is a lot compared to that big dividend yield (and a 55-year dividend growth streak).

In fact, Altria is doing exactly what a financially healthy company in its position should do—return massive cash to shareholders (in the form of big dividends and share repurchases).

Altria Risks:

Investors should also be aware of the risks associated with investing in Altria.

  • Declining Cigarette Volumes: Altria’s core smokeable segment (88% of revenue) faces ongoing US shipment declines (~8–10% annually) from health awareness and illicit trade. Nonetheless, Altria continues to offset this with price increases (loyal and addicted customers continue to show little elasticity to prices rising faster than inflation, as described earlier).

  • Regulatory and Litigation Pressures: FDA actions, potential bans (e.g., menthol cigarettes), and lawsuits could impose fines, marketing restrictions, and/or import bans on products like NJOY ACE (which has been prohibited since March 2025).

  • Economic and Consumer Shifts: Inflation, tariffs, and reduced disposable income may drive downtrading to cheaper/illicit options. For example, a pack of Marlboro Red cigarettes now costs $15-$19 in the city of New York, whereas it only cost around $1.00-$1.50 40 years ago.

  • Supply Chain Disruptions: According to Altria, geopolitical tensions, climate change, and tobacco sourcing volatility also pose significant risks to the business.

  • Management Changes: Altria CEO Billy Gifford recently announced his retirement (effective May 2026). He will be replaced by current CFO Salvatore Mancuso. Additionally, the Altria board has elected Heather Newman as CFO. While the transition is expected to be smooth, the changes do introduce risks worth monitoring.

Altria Takeaways

For a company with a wide moat that generates impressive free cash flows, Altria’s shares are significantly inexpensive, likely in significant part due to its “sin-stock” discount (i.e. a lot of institutions won’t buy tobacco products and thereby drive down buying pressure and the price). And Altria is doing the exact right thing in this situation by returning huge cash to shareholders in the form of massive, qualified dividends and share repurchases. The shares have also just recently pulled back in recent months, thereby sweetening the deal. If you are an unconstrained, income-focused investor, Altria shares are absolutely worth considering.

3 More Big Yields Worth Considering

In addition to Altria, it would be remiss to not at least consider a few more big-yield opportunities from the list of 40, so let’s briefly get into that.

PIMCO’s Access Income Fund (PAXS), 11.4% Yield

PIMCO is the premier big-yield bond fund manager, and PAXS is an attractive CEF that stands out. Not only does it offer a big yield (paid monthly), but it also trades at a reasonably small 0.9% premium to net asset value (attractive for a top PIMCO fund).

It also uses a healthy dose of leverage, or borrowed money (currently ~38%), to help magnify the income produced by its largely bond holdings (and PIMCO gets lower institutional borrowing rates and is much more operationally efficient at borrowing than most individuals).

PAXS also purchases bonds that are virtually impossible for a little guy (non-institutional investor) to purchase. Further still, PAXS is a relatively newer PIMCO fund; it has never reduced its distribution, and it has avoided a lot of the legacy derivatives (e.g., interest rate swaps) that older funds used (and to some extent still have on their books) to survive the post-covid interest rate volatility. It’s also doing just fine covering the distribution with investment income as compared to other PIMCO funds (as you can see in the graphic below).

If you are an income-focused investor, PAXS shares are down (see early table) and worth considering.

Trinity Capital (TRIN), 13.9% Yield

Trinity is a big-dividend Business Development Company (“BDC”) and the shares have recently sold off thereby making for a more attractive entry point. And whereas a lot of other BDCs focus on “old” economic sectors, Trinity’s niche focus on growth and technology sectors gives its investors differentiated big-yield exposure. Not only is the outsized dividend yield attractive, but it’s generated from “newer” parts of the economy—arguably providing significantly more long-term growth potential.

It does come with risks, including potential pain when the current growth-sector rally sells off (potentially significantly harder than more value-focused BDCs). However, overall, it’s an attractive, big-yield BDC that is absolutely worth considering. I own it in my prudently diversified, long-term, income-focused portfolio, and I wrote more about Trinity here.

Main Street Capital (MAIN), Yield: 5.3%

Another BDC, investors love to look at Main Street Capital’s relatively high price-to-book value and immediately declare it an overvalued bubble. But what many investors don’t realize is it’s a fundamentally different type of BDC with its steady net asset value (“NAV”) gains (while peers stay relatively flat—or worse). So while some thumb their nose at Main Street’s mere 5.3% dividend yield (puny in the BDC space), they fail to recognize the steady dividend growth, plus the powerful supplemental dividends, as well as the many other attractive qualities that set it apart (i.e., low debt, strong liquidity, above-average pipeline, healthy portfolio, efficient operations, and macro resilience).

I review all of that (and the big risks) and then conclude with my strong opinion on investing in Main Street Capital in this report.

S&P 500 Index Fund (SPY)

As mentioned earlier, a lot of investors believe the simplest and best income strategy is to buy a low-cost S&P 500 ETF and then just sell a few shares every month or quarter to create income (i.e., build your own dividend from price appreciation). Of course this comes with the volatility problem. Specifically, the market gets extremely volatile at times, and if you are forced to sell shares at “the bottom” of a selloff, you will effectively sell low and lock in losses (whereas many dividend investors sleep better at night knowing their big dividends keep rolling in even when market prices get volatile). Nonetheless, this can be a compelling strategy to consider for some of your investment dollars (diversification can be an extremely good thing), but of course it depends on your personal situation. And you can read more about my opinion on that in this report, "S&P 500: Cloud-AI Bubble, Huge Pain Ahead".

The Bottom Line:

Don’t ever rate an investment opportunity by its big yield alone. Rather, consider also the fundamental qualities and risks of the business or fund, and consider also the tax consequences based on your own individual situation (all of these factors can have dramatic impacts on your results).

Altria stands out for its big, well-covered, qualified dividend—a type of income source that has advantages versus other categories, as discussed (not to mention its shares are down and the valuation is attractive). However, that’s not to say attractive opportunities don’t exist in the other big yield categories too—they do!

And although not my biggest positions, I do currently own all four of the specific big-yield examples (e.g., Altria, PIMCO Access Income Fund, Trinity Capital, and Main Street Capital) within my prudently diversified, 25-position, 9.6% aggregate yield, high-income portfolio.

However, at the end of the day, you need to do what is right for you. Be smart, people; invest according to your own personal situation.

data.title: Ashtead Group: Still In A Show Me Phase
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Ashtead Group (ASHTF), for which I recommended a hold rating as the near-term growth acceleration potential was limited, albeit the long-term outlook was still very healthy, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I believe ASHTF has shown signs of stabilization, and there is visibility to earnings growth eventually accelerating, particularly through mega-project ramp-up. That said, the translation from pipeline strength to reported earnings remains slow. The setup is improving, but ASHTF is still in a “show me” phase.

2Q 2026 Earnings

ASHTF’s Q2 results showed stabilization, not a full recovery, in my view. Reported revenue came in at ~$2.96 billion, up just 1% y/y, which is better than a decline but still doesn’t signal any re-acceleration strength. Group rental revenue paints a similar story, growing just 1% y/y to ~$2.76 billion. To be fair, we have to adjust for the hurricane impact, which would bring the growth closer to 3%. Overall, the core demand strength is still positive; it’s just that it is not accelerating (momentum is more muted). By mix, North America General Tool total revenue was up 1% y/y, and Specialty was flat at $970 million, but again, if we adjust for the hurricane impact, Specialty rental revenue would have grown 5% y/y. The UK continues to be a drag, with rental revenue down 2% y/y on a constant currency basis.

However, profitability did not move in line with the top line. While revenue was up 1% y/y, adj. EBITDA was down 2% y/y to ~$1.38 billion, and margins compressed 140 bps to 46.6%. The reason was both mix and cost. Growth in lower-margin mega projects, higher ancillary revenue, and fleet repositioning costs all weighed on profitability. At the adj. EBIT line, it was down 4% y/y to $789 million, and adj. profit before tax fell 4% to $656 million. Adj. EPS still grew 1% y/y to ~$1.17, but this was largely due to buybacks.

Underlying improvements

I'm still "hold" rated, but there are real good takeaways that give me even more conviction that the mega-project narrative I mentioned before is moving in the right direction. A few months ago, I noted that non-residential completions were running ahead of starts, and that would eventually lead to a volume “air pocket.” As the backlog of work gets run through the P&L and new demand doesn’t come in, at some point, there will not be enough active work to drive growth. That dynamic appears to have turned the corner. A few data points support this. Firstly, management commented that they believe the market has reached an equilibrium. Secondly, the Dodge Momentum Index is at near all-time highs. Lastly, internal quotations (I take this as a proxy for tendering activity) and reservations (an increase here tells us that projects that were in the “quotation” phase have started work) also point to a recovery ahead.

Mega projects

I have been talking about this mega-project narrative for a while now, and it has mostly been about the scale and potential of it. But for this update, I'd like to touch more on the mix and why the margin drag today is not a problem.

On the former, while the headline pipeline value is still at $1.307 trillion for FY26-28 (flat vs. my last update), the mix is getting better. Management noted they are seeing notable growth in data center investment, which is offsetting weaker areas like EV/battery plants. This shift is very important because data centers tend to be highly equipment-intensive and complex projects. This is where ASHTF’s ability to cross-sell both its general and specialty tools comes into play. For such complex projects, it makes sense for the project owner to just deal with one player for all the required equipment (i.e., an integrated solutions provider like ASHTF that can provide a wide range of solutions from climate control and backup power to even cabling).

The bear case for this mega-project narrative is the margin drag that comes with it. Management did acknowledge that early-stage mega projects are dilutive during the ramp-up phase due to mobilization costs, but they reach parity with group margins at maturity. The way to think about this is that, before the projects start, there is basically no revenue coming in, but all the costs associated with moving the equipment hit the P&L. But once the project starts, the equipment stays on-site (no need for moving the fleet back and forth), and with deployed field service technicians directly on these massive sites, ASHTF ensures efficient maintenance, which means less downtime (and therefore lower repair costs). Essentially, what we are seeing today is that margins are still weighed down as many projects are still in the "installation phase" (i.e., equipment moving in), and once the project continues to advance, we should see a nice ramp-up in profitability.

Bearish Factors

All the above is good and supports the long-term bull case, but the reality for investors is that current earnings growth is limited. As much as one can spin about mega projects and whatnot, headline rental revenue grew just 1% y/y, which is not bad, but just weak relative to history. The fact that management reaffirmed FY26 rental revenue guidance of 0 to 4% y/y tells you everything you need to know about the slope of the recovery. There is no V-shape coming. The bulls will point to the Dodge Momentum Index that sits near record highs, but remember that management explicitly stated that planning activity typically takes 12 to 24 months to translate into revenue. This means the surge in the Dodge Momentum Index you see today will only hit the P&L in terms of revenue growth in ~18 months' time.

On the point of margin, knowing that margin would ramp up is good, but as mentioned, it takes time for revenue to ramp up. Meanwhile, ASHTF has to deal with the higher internal repair costs as a larger share of the fleet rolls off warranty (this caused a 140 bps EBITDA margin drag in Q2 2026). Management noted this headwind to persist for another 6 quarters (1.5 years) as ASHTF laps the heavy capex cycles of FY23 and FY24. In other words, even if revenue stabilizes, and we see some revenue ramp from the mega project pipeline, margin expansion will be structurally hard to achieve in the near term.

Valuation

The market appears to agree with my view that near-term EBITDA growth will be rather muted (as you can see from the consensus estimates above). If you are playing the long-term game and can hold through volatility, you may justify a buy rating today since the mega projects should eventually flow into the P&L, and earnings growth should accelerate accordingly. But from now till then, one has to deal with sentiment fluctuations, especially if ASHTF misses consensus estimates (could be due to a ramp-up in moving equipment, which hits the P&L, and the fleet rolling off the warranty period). If you recall what happened back in Q2/3 2025, when ASHTF missed earnings, the stock price was down sharply.

Hence, my view is still the same: that ASTHF is in a “show me” period. It has to show the market that earnings growth can indeed accelerate; if not, the stock price likely stays range-bound.

Conclusion

I believe ASHTF’s long-term fundamentals are intact, but the near-term earnings growth profile is still muted given the weak rental growth and margin headwinds. Mega projects are real, the pipeline is credible, and margins should improve as projects mature, but that takes time. Until revenue growth meaningfully reaccelerates and margin pressure eases, I expect the stock to remain range-bound.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vera Bradley Returns To Its Core, But It Remains Fairly Challenged
data.description:
Vera Bradley (VRA) released 3Q25 results.

The quarter does not show a change in trend, with continued challenges on the topline (albeit sequentially "improving"), operational losses (close to breakeven if adjusted) in one of the company's strongest quarters seasonally, and significant impairments.

However, it does provide more information, particularly on the strategy of the new management team. From what I understood, the goal is almost a complete return to the pre-rebranding period, with a focus on the core, historical Vera Bradley identity and products.

Overall, the name remains in great flux, and although it owned significant brand power in the past, today it is simply too risky to consider for investment, even at these lower prices. I maintain a Hold on Vera.

Strategic return to the core

Before going into the results (which I believe are still mainly reflective of previous mistakes), I believe the highest-ranked data point from the results was the comments from Vera's new CEO/Chairman on the call regarding strategy in product, marketing, price points, etc.

We should remember that Vera's current problems got worse (the company was already underperforming) after a massive rebranding in mid-2024, which hurt the company's most loyal customers by almost completely removing the company's core designs and product characteristics. This led to a sales collapse and eventually to the ousting of the company's CEO that led the rebranding.

In the 3Q25 call, the company's interim executive Chairman took a long time to explain that Vera will basically return to its pre-rebranding state.

The core positioning of the brand will be joyful, colorful, and smart value (i.e., accessible prices for good quality, without discounting). This is basically what had defined Vera Bradley in the past.

On product, the company will focus on core, 'hero' products, with a decrease in SKUs to focus assortment on these hero products. These include legacy winners like the Vera Totes, Glenna Satchel, or the Original 100 Bag. Products will return to a focus on prints, made of cotton (rebranding tried to move products towards leather), and usability (large bags, not fashion-linked miny bags).

Potentially shifting from a pre-branding company, Vera Bradley will now focus on digital advertising, compared to a previous focus on more analogic channels (catalogs, TV, for example).

3Q25 results

Now moving back to the results, the company touted the quarter as an improvement from the tragic 1H25 (both with revenue down ~25%), but I believe the trend, so far, continues.

This quarter, sales were down much less, 'only' 12%, with direct sales down 5% and indirect sales (wholesale) down 30%. Although the sequential improvement seems real, we need to compare it with previous quarters last year. In 3Q24, Vera had posted sales down 27% (excluding Pura Vida), compared to sales down ~15% in 1H24. That is, the comparable quarter for 3Q25 was worse than those for 1H25. Therefore, it is hard to determine if the trend is improving or not.

Moving to margins, the company posted large operational losses ($12 million), but at least $10 million came from impairments ($6 million in inventories, probably from the rebrand, and $4 million from TV advertising credits that will not be used, for some hard-to-understand reason). These impairments have to be taken with attention, because in the future the company might post improved margins thanks to using impaired stocks, either inventories or advertising. For example, if in 2Q26 next year Vera uses those TV advertising credits, it will not record them in SG&A but will enjoy the demand boost. The same can be said of previously impaired inventories, which boost gross margins in previous quarters (it's like selling inventories at $0 cost).

On an adjusted basis, margins were better than last year. The operating loss was much closer to breakeven ($2 million loss), much less than last year's $9 million. Gross margins (ex inventory writeoffs) were 51%, or $32 million in (adjusted) gross profits, versus $38 million last year. This was more than offset by SG&A falling $11 million on an adjusted basis (adding back the TV impairments).

Overall, we could observe an improvement in the sales trend (considering that the company returned to the product their clients were requesting) and potentially even in margins, even if heavily adjusted. However, more is needed to confirm this.

Financial situation

Given the large losses (adjusted or not) and the fact that this was one of the strong seasonal quarters for Vera (see chart below), we have to consider the financial strength of Vera to sustain losses during its turnaround.

The company currently has about $11 million in cash and $10 million in debt, plus access to a credit facility for $65 million.

The quarter, when adjusted, posted an operational loss of $2 million, and CFO before working capital was negative by $6 million. Assuming similar losses forward (somewhat bullish given that 1H has lower leverage, but also assuming no improvement in operations), this implies $25 million in cash losses per year. With the above liquidity, the company should be fine, albeit this is hard to establish fully in the midst of such changes in structure and strategy.

Too risky for investment

Vera Bradley trades at about $56 million ($2 by 28 million shares). This seems very little compared to pre-pandemic years with operating income above $30 million, some even above $50 million.

However, the company has been on a consistent trend of lower lows for almost a decade. It was not only the rebranding that caused Vera's problems. The company was already struggling, and the rebranding simply worsened the situation. Therefore, it is not clear that with a return to the previous strategy things will simply improve.

Although financing exists, I believe the worst downside risk for the stock is the need to finance cash outflows as long as it maintains operating and even EBITDA losses. The risk is that the re-re-branding is not enough to push sales upwards to the minimum profitability level.

On the upside, the company's market cap is low in absolute terms, and not so long ago (5 years), it was doing more EBITDA than its current market cap. If Vera showed some positive quarters (and with such terrible comps in 4Q24 and onwards, it may be 'easy'), then the stock price could react violently to the upside.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold, on the basis that the name is too risky for investment, but I also consider that some more speculative readers might find the name interesting.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Prediction Markets Are Upending U.S. Gambling: What It Could Mean For Non-Investment Grade Credit
data.description:
A 2024 court ruling allowed prediction markets to operate as federally regulated event contract exchanges. With nationwide reach, these products are pressuring state-regulated sports books, lotteries and casinos - raising new considerations for non-investment-grade issuers and investors.

Before the birth of the United States, individual colonies ran lotteries to pay for all manner of civic projects. For the next 250 years, difficult political decisions around gambling regulation remained the purview of the states.

This generated a patchwork of regulation, with states like Nevada taking a more liberal view of gambling activity of all sorts than, say, Texas. However, technological advances, recent court rulings and a laissez faire presidential administration threaten to disrupt this landscape in important and far-reaching ways.

Court Win and Election Change the Betting Landscape

In 2024, upstart online “prediction market” operator Kalshi (KALSHI) earned a court win that allowed it to offer “event contracts” on that year’s elections, with the court ruling that such contracts were financial swaps subject to federal Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regulation, not gambling products subject to state regulation.

As background, these contracts are freely traded between third-party market participants on an exchange, and pay either $1 or $0 depending on whether an event - say, the election of President Trump - occurs or not. The trading price can be interpreted as implied odds of an event occurring, with a 30-cent contract corresponding to 30% odds.

While 2024 court battles focused on political prediction markets, the arrival of an administration that is generally supportive of financial markets - including novel products in the crypto and prediction market spaces - created an opening for Kalshi and its competitors to test the limits of the “swap” definition.

These companies now offer event contracts on sports outcomes and even events that resemble player “props” and “parlays” (for example, bets tied to the “event” of U.S. football quarterback Patrick Mahomes passing for more than 300 yards).

In the eyes of the user, event contracts and their economics can look very similar to online sports bets offered by DraftKings (DKNG) or FanDuel (DUEL), despite being governed by a completely different regulatory framework.

Differing Regulation Favors the Upstarts

Federal regulation of these markets has allowed their operation in all 50 states, including the roughly 20 states covering half of the U.S. population that have no or limited online sports betting.

This has completely sidelined states with respect to important decisions around gambling legalization (including the number and type of operators), accessibility (prediction markets are available via online or mobile apps to anyone 18 or over, versus 21 or over for gambling), safety/responsible gaming and taxation (prediction markets avoid paying certain state taxes that are applicable to lottery and casino operators).

While we expect the U.S. Supreme Court to eventually rule on the difficult jurisdictional and definitional questions involved here, that decision may take many years.

In the meantime, this nearly instantaneous nationwide legalization of a gambling-adjacent product threatens to disrupt existing gambling markets. It may also lead to a proliferation of gambling legalization across the country, as states compete to retain potential taxable revenues within taxable gambling products.

Recent announcements by FanDuel and DraftKings to enter the prediction market space with hundreds of millions in start-up spending also seem to suggest they are chasing the large opportunities associated with significant market change.

New Competition Could Require Investor Vigilance

We believe non-investment grade issuers in the lottery space, which until now have enjoyed virtually no legal gambling competition in some jurisdictions, may be negatively affected.

Meanwhile, the impact on regional gaming operators is likely to be mixed depending on their footprint and whether they can capitalize on any subsequent legalizations.

For investors, this will require even greater investment vigilance and discretion. For the moment, all we can arguably predict is that disruption could be on its way.

Sports Betting in the USA

Where gambling on sports is allowed, and where it could be on the way

Source: USA Today, as of December 6, 2025.

Investment decisions and the appropriateness of this material should be made based on an investor's individual objectives and circumstances and in consultation with his or her advisors. Information is obtained from sources deemed reliable, but there is no representation or warranty as to its accuracy, completeness or reliability. All information is current as of the date of this material and is subject to change without notice. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types.

The views expressed herein include those of Neuberger Berman’s Private Wealth Investment Group. The Private Wealth Investment Group analyzes market and economic indicators to develop asset allocation strategies. The Private Wealth Investment Group works in partnership with the Office of the CIO. The Private Wealth Investment Group also consults regularly with portfolio managers and investment officers across the firm. The views of the Private Wealth Investment Group may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole and Neuberger Berman advisers and portfolio managers may take contrary positions to the views of the Private Wealth Investment Group.
